The surgeon uses a 10mm telescope with a 30-degree lens to perform laparoscopic surgery on a 34-year-old patient with bilateral endometriomas and a small anterior fibroid. The transvaginal ultrasound shows the rectum adhered to the posterior uterine wall. The surgeon drains the cysts, separates the right fallopian tube from the ovary, and releases the ovary from pelvic adhesions. The chocolate cyst is aspirated, revealing thick, pasty contents. Tubal patency is tested, confirming both tubes are patent.

The surgeon examines the anterior view, revealing the bladder adherent to the uterus with multiple nodules present. The technique involves hooking up the ovaries using a non-absorbable suture on a straight needle, leaving the cyst intact for later removal. The surgeon then works in the pouch of Douglas, where the rectum is stuck to the vagina. Using three-dimensional laparoscopy, the surgeon identifies the right pararectal space and aims to shave nodules from the uterus without affecting the rectum, carefully separating the rectum from the uterus.

The surgeon successfully releases the rectum from the uterus, allowing it to descend. The next step involves locating and isolating the right ureter, which is identified amidst surrounding tissue. Using a bisect instrument, the surgeon excises nodules from the uterus and the right utero sacral ligament to reduce recurrence rates.

The surgeon palpates the tissue to identify endometriotic nodules, excising only the diseased areas while preserving normal tissue. Using a bisect instrument, the surgeon coagulates and cuts simultaneously, efficiently removing nodules near the ureter. The three-D laparoscopy provides enhanced depth perception, allowing precise dissection and excision of nodules on the peritoneum above the ureter.

The surgeon performs a Michelin test by injecting water and air into the pelvis, revealing a serosal tear on the rectum. Using a 3-0 polyglecting suture, the surgeon repairs the tear by suturing the serosal areas together. Next, the surgeon proceeds with a cystectomy, carefully separating the cyst wall from the ovary using vasopressin for hydrodissection. The cyst wall is gently teased away using sharp and blunt dissection, preserving as much ovarian tissue as possible to maintain the patient's fertility.

The surgeon completes the cystectomy and moves to the anterior wall of the uterus, identifying endometriotic nodules on the left utero-sacral ligament and bladder. Using scissors, the surgeon carefully excises the nodules to avoid burning the bladder, demonstrating precision in removing disease while preserving normal tissue. The surgeon then excises disease from the round ligament and continues to the right utero-sacral ligament, using a bisect for dissection away from the bladder and scissors for areas closer to the bladder, ensuring thorough removal of endometriotic tissue.

The surgeon uses sharp dissection to excise the disease, employing a grasper to gently pull and remove the affected tissue. A small fibroid is visible and is enucleated and removed. The defect left by the fibroid removal is repaired using a polyglycine one suture, with an inverting baseball suture technique to close the defect completely. The surgeon then repairs the ovarian defect, performing an ovariopexy to secure the ovary to the peritoneum using a polyglycine two-oh suture.

The surgeon identifies and excises a nodule using a bisect instrument. Another nodule located on top of the right ureter is carefully excised with sharp scissors to avoid using diathermy. The surgeon meticulously removes visible disease to improve the patient's chances of spontaneous pregnancy. Anti-adhesion barriers are placed, and the uterus is observed to be non-retroverted after removing the uterine manipulator.

The surgeon uses a hook and suction instrument to open the peritoneum over the sacral promontory, exposing the posterior part of the uterus and the right utero sacral ligament. The space is opened to separate the rectum from the peritoneum, aiming to reach the levator ani for posterior mesh placement to support the uterovaginal prolapse. The surgeon clears fat tissue overlying the rectum and dissects the left lateral pararectal space down to the levator ani, utilizing three-D laparoscopy for enhanced depth perception.

The surgeon performs slow dissection around the medial rectal artery, avoiding injury while accessing the levator ani muscle. The rectum is visible in the center as the surgeon opens the right medial pararectal space, aiming to reach the puborectalis muscle for mesh attachment. The subtotal hysterectomy is completed, removing the uterine body and leaving the cervix for mesh attachment. Fat tissue is excised to expose the rectum for posterior support, and a non-absorbable suture is placed into the left puborectalis muscle to attach the mesh.

The surgeon ties down a non-absorbable suture, securing the mesh in place with multiple knots. The left stitch is placed, followed by the right stitch into the right puborectalis muscle, attaching the mesh for posterior support. The mesh sits well on the rectum, providing support. The surgeon then dissects the bladder anteriorly, reaching near the trigone to correct any cystocele, using a fine non-absorbable suture to attach the mesh.

The surgeon places two stitches on the lateral side to attach the mesh to the vaginal wall, ensuring the mesh sits below the bladder. The mesh is then sutured down and attached to the sacral promontory using a number two stitch on the longitudinal ligament, completing the sacral colpopexy.

The surgeon performs a radical hysterectomy, focusing on de-roofing the ureteric tunnel using harmonic instruments. The left uterine artery has been cut, and the ascending branch of the uterine artery is visible. Careful manipulation is required to avoid injuring the ureter and causing fistulas.

The surgeon uses the ERBI BiClan, a precise smart bipolar coagulative instrument, to release tissue from the knuckle of the ureter. The bipolar device is used to coagulate and cut the tissue, allowing for careful dissection near the ureter. The procedure is performed in the Yabuki space, medial to the ureter, with the aid of three-dimensional laparoscopy.

The surgeon uses harmonics to carefully de-roof the ureteric tunnel on the right side, releasing the ureter from surrounding parametrial tissue. The ureter is isolated at the knuckle, a critical area where it shifts and turns, requiring precise dissection and coagulation. The bladder is released from the cervix, and the ureter is completely dissected, facilitated by the three-D laparoscopy.
